---
title: "Cohort Assembly"
author: "Tyler Tong"
date: today
format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: true
    number-sections: true
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 4
    toc-location: left
    code-fold: false
engine: knitr
---

# Cohort Table
```{R}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
library(tidyverse)
library(readr)
# get data
data <- read_tsv("~/AiReadi/AiReadiSummer2026/Data/participants.tsv")
cgmdata <- read_tsv("~/AiReadi/AiReadiSummer2026/Data/cgm.tsv")
wearabledata <- read_tsv("~/AiReadi/AiReadiSummer2026/Data/wearable.tsv")
# remove unneeded variables
cgmdata <- cgmdata |> select(person_id, average_glucose_level_mg_dl)
wearabledata <- wearabledata |> select(person_id, average_daily_activity)
# join data by person_id
data <- data |> left_join(cgmdata, by = join_by(person_id))
data <- data |> left_join(wearabledata, by = join_by(person_id))
# print
glimpse(data)
```
# Mini Dataset Analysis
```{R}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
library(tidyverse)
library(readr)
data <- read_tsv("~/AiReadi/AiReadiSummer2026/Data/participants.tsv")
ggplot(data, aes(y = study_group)) + geom_bar()
```
```{R}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
library(tidyverse)
library(readr)
data <- read_tsv("~/AiReadi/AiReadiSummer2026/Data/participants.tsv")
data <- data |> count(clinical_site, study_group)
ggplot(data, aes(x = clinical_site, y = study_group, fill = n)) + geom_tile()
```
```{R}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
library(tidyverse)
library(readr)
library(purrr)

data <- read_tsv("~/AiReadi/AiReadiSummer2026/Data/participants.tsv")

countcgm <- data |> count(wearable_blood_glucose)
countwearable <- data |> count(wearable_activity_monitor)
countclinical <- data |> count(clinical_data)
countcgmwearable <- data |> count(wearable_blood_glucose, wearable_activity_monitor)
countall <- data |> count(wearable_blood_glucose, wearable_activity_monitor, clinical_data)

counts <- transmute(countcgm, cgm = map_dbl(n, 1))
counts <- counts |> cross_join(transmute(countwearable, wearable = map_dbl(n, 1)))
counts <- counts |> cross_join(transmute(countclinical, clinical = map_dbl(n, 1)))
counts <- counts |> cross_join(transmute(countcgmwearable, cgmwearable = map_dbl(n, 1)))
counts <- counts |> cross_join(transmute(countcgmwearable, cgmwearableclinical = map_dbl(n, 1)))

glimpse(counts)
```
```{R}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
library(readr)
library(tidyverse)

df <- read_tsv("~/AiReadi/AiReadiSummer2026/Data/participants.tsv", show_col_types = FALSE)

modality_counts <- df %>%
  select(
    cardiac_ecg, 
    clinical_data, 
    environment, 
    retinal_flio,
    retinal_oct, 
    retinal_octa, 
    retinal_photography,
    wearable_activity_monitor, 
    wearable_blood_glucose
  ) |>
  mutate(across(everything(), as.logical)) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = everything(), 
    names_to = "Modality", 
    values_to = "Is_Available"
  ) |>
  filter(Is_Available == TRUE) |>
  count(Modality, name = "Frequency") %>%
  mutate(Modality = fct_reorder(Modality, Frequency))

ggplot(modality_counts, aes(x = Frequency, y = Modality, fill = Modality)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE, alpha = 0.85) +
  geom_text(aes(label = Frequency), hjust = -0.2, size = 4) +
  scale_x_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15)))
```
# Mini vs Full
- Mini only has 100 participants, while the full dataset has 2k+.
- Both the full and mini have the same study groups and clinical sites.
- The full data set has some gaps in availability, but the mini has full coverage.
- any code written for the mini data set should run the same on the full data set.
# Analysis Cohorts

## CGM Cohort

- All participants with CGM data are in this cohort.
- Contains 2245 participants.
- Represents all study groups.
- Can be used to study how glucose changes in different people who have different conditions, as well as how glucose affects clinical data.
- 80 of the participants in this cohort do not have wearable data, so it can't be used to study how lifestyle affects glucose.

## CGM + Wearable Cohort

- Participants who have CGM and wearable data are in this cohort.
- Contains 2145 participants.
- Can be used to study how glucose responds to lifestyle changes, as well as how that may affect clinical data.
- Cannot be used as a representative sample of the real population, as it has selection bias towards those who provided wearable data.
